metric_value: HP leads network management market in both market share and mindshare; large installed base of hardware and software; worldwide presence; strong marketing
notes: Aberdeen acknowledges HP's dominant position in 1997

metric_value: Tivoli completely rewrote TME 10 NetView 5.0 to fully exploit Windows APIs; designed for branch office NT environments with Mid-Level Manager agent architecture
notes: Tivoli's differentiation via full Windows-native rewrite

metric_value: Mid-Level Manager agents collect data on branch office devices and filter only relevant data up to centralized server; Central NetView + Regional NetView + MLM agents
notes: Key architectural differentiator vs HP NNM's full implementation requirement

metric_value: HP NNM requires full package licensed and deployed at each location to be monitored; processes all event data unfiltered; leads to wasted bandwidth and system delays
notes: Aberdeen's core technical criticism of NNM

metric_value: TME 10 NetView was first network management product to adopt Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP); reduces false alarms from users turning off terminals
Dynamically updated groups of nodes (e.g. 'down routers' 'all printers'); automatic addition when router goes down; improves exception reporting and proactive management
notes: Key differentiator for proactive network management

metric_value: HP NNM web browser is static text-only HTML format; requires third-party supplier separately; text-based table reports time-consuming to decipher
notes: Aberdeen criticizes HP's failure to modernize web interface

metric_value: NetView uses Netscape browser with combination of Java / JavaScript / HTML formats; view-only at publication time; full security/remote management planned by end 1998
TME 10 NetView fully exploits Windows APIs; drop-down menus parallel Microsoft Office suite; reduces training time; NNM navigation time-consuming with excessive windows
SAM revises multiple locations simultaneously with single button press; NNM requires manual updates at each location; SAM ships on Unix; usable from Unix to execute on NT
HP reached success then lost momentum; competitors filled vacuum; complacency and lack of investment in product R&D caused NNM to fall behind in user-friendliness / efficiency / breadth
notes: Aberdeen's critical assessment of HP's competitive posture

metric_value: HP recently announced addition of 200-300 new sales staff for OpenView — described as a small step but not addressing root problem
TME 10 NetView advantage: Windows-native GUI; Office-parallel drop-down menus; drag-and-drop; sidebar navigation history; reduces training time and cost
TME 10 NetView advantage: prebuilt report formats launchable with single click; dynamic event updates without user intervention; SmartSets for proactive exception reporting
TME 10 NetView advantage: Regional NetView small footprint for branch offices; fraction of cost of full implementation; Mid-Level Managers reduce hardware/admin overhead
notes: Three of four evaluation criteria; HP NNM more cost-effective for standalone workgroups

metric_value: TME 10 NetView advantage: first to adopt DHCP; reduces false alarms from powered-off devices; distinguishes blue (off) from red (broken)
Integrated network and systems management is a 'two-horse race' between IBM Tivoli TME and Computer Associates CA-Unicenter at enterprise level; HP and Sun lead below enterprise level
Released September 1997
Aberdeen predicts NNM destined to fall further behind as Tivoli moves forward unless HP redirects commitment to customer needs
notes: Explicit negative prediction for HP NNM competitive position

metric_value: Aberdeen highly recommends TME 10 NetView for managers evaluating cost-effective robust scalable NT management options; leadership role in market
HP discontinued NNM version 6.4/6.41 effective December 31 2007; final version was 7.5x discontinued May 2010; product line sold to MicroFocus then OpenText
notes: Sources: HPE end-of-life notice; HP OpenView Wikipedia; https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/HP_OpenView

metric_value: TME 10 NetView evolved into IBM Tivoli NetView; Tivoli brand became dominant IBM systems management platform through 2000s; NetView for z/OS still active as of 2026 for mainframe management
notes: IBM Tivoli NetView for z/OS remains active product; IBM Tivoli brand was broadly successful; Aberdeen's recommendation proved prescient

metric_value: Hewlett-Packard split into HP Inc. and Hewlett Packard Enterprise in 2015; HP Software division (including OpenView heritage products) sold to MicroFocus in 2017 and then to OpenText in 2022
TME 10 NetView supports: Unix AIX / Digital UX / Solaris / NT on Alpha and Intel
HP NNM supports: Solaris / HP-UX / NT Server environments
Tivoli suffers from inadequate support staff due to substantial recent growth in demand for TME 10 NetView; neither company has strong advantage in sales/support
IBM acquisition prior to v5.0 brought additional technical resources and expertise providing catalyst for significant enhancements from TME 10 NetView version 4.1
notes: Aberdeen notes positive effect of IBM ownership on product quality
